items.correct: Protease enzymes break proteins down into amino acids; amylase digests starch and lipase digests fats.
items.misconception: Amylase digests proteins into amino acids.
items.random_wrong: Bile digests proteins into amino acids.
items.why: Students mix up which enzyme acts on which food group.

items.q: What is fibre's role if it isn't digested?
items.correct: Fibre adds bulk that helps muscles move food through the gut, preventing constipation, even though it isn't broken down.
items.misconception: Fibre is a nutrient that is digested for energy.
items.random_wrong: Fibre has no purpose because the body can't digest it.
items.why: Students assume anything not digested has no dietary value.

items.q: Does a high BMI always mean someone is unhealthy?
items.correct: BMI is only an indicator; a very muscular person can have a high BMI without excess fat, so it must be used with care.
items.misconception: A high BMI always proves a person has too much body fat.
items.random_wrong: BMI measures how much sugar is in the blood.
items.why: Students treat BMI as a precise direct measure of body fat.

items.q: The enzyme that breaks down protein is:
items.why: It's protease. “amylase” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Most absorption of digested food occurs in the:
items.why: It's small intestine. “stomach” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A lack of vitamin D in children can cause:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: rickets. “scurvy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Fibre is important in the diet because it:
items.correct: helps move food through the gut and prevents constipation
items.misconception: provides most of the body's energy
items.why: The answer examiners want is: helps move food through the gut and prevents constipation. “provides most of the body's energy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A person has a mass of 80 kg and a height of 2 m. Calculate their BMI in kg/m^2.
items.why: The real figure is 20 kg/m^2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
